exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 4: Complete these definitions with the correct participle of the verb given in brackets

promptParts.before: A gateway is an interface
promptParts.after: (enable) dissimilar networks to communicate.

questions.grammarRule: The interface is the active agent that enables communication (= 'an interface which enables...'), so the active participle is needed: 'enabling'.

promptParts.before: A bridge is a hardware and software combination
promptParts.after: (use) to connect the same type of networks.

questions.grammarRule: The combination doesn't use itself — it IS used (by someone) to connect networks (= 'a combination which is used...'), so the passive participle is needed: 'used'.

promptParts.before: A backbone is network transmission path
promptParts.after: (handle) major data traffic.

questions.grammarRule: The path is the active agent that handles traffic (= 'a path which handles...'), so the active participle is needed: 'handling'.

promptParts.before: A router is a special computer
promptParts.after: (direct) messages when several networks are linked.

questions.grammarRule: The router is the active agent that directs messages (= 'a computer which directs...'), so the active participle is needed: 'directing'.

promptParts.before: A network is a number of computers and peripherals
promptParts.after: (link) together.

questions.grammarRule: The computers and peripherals don't link themselves — they ARE linked together (by cabling/software) (= 'a number of things which are linked...'), so the passive participle is needed: 'linked'.

promptParts.before: A LAN is a network
promptParts.after: (connect) computers over a small distance such as within the company.

questions.grammarRule: The network is the active agent that connects the computers (= 'a network which connects...'), so the active participle is needed: 'connecting'.

promptParts.before: A server is a powerful computer
promptParts.after: (store) many programs shared by all the clients in the network.

questions.grammarRule: The server is the active agent that stores programs (= 'a computer which stores...'), so the active participle is needed: 'storing'.

promptParts.before: A server is a powerful computer storing many programs
promptParts.after: by all the clients in the network.

questions.grammarRule: The programs don't share themselves — they ARE shared (by/among the clients) (= 'programs which are shared...'), so the passive participle is needed: 'shared'.

promptParts.before: A client is a network computer
promptParts.after: (use) for accessing a service on a server.

questions.grammarRule: The client computer doesn't use itself — it IS used (by a person) for accessing a service (= 'a computer which is used...'), so the passive participle is needed: 'used'.

promptParts.before: A thin client is a simple computer
promptParts.after: (comprise) a processor and memory, display, keyboard, mouse and hard drives only.

questions.grammarRule: The computer is the active agent that comprises (contains) these parts (= 'a computer which comprises...'), so the active participle is needed: 'comprising'.

promptParts.before: A hub is an electronic device
promptParts.after: (connect) all the data cabling in a network.

questions.grammarRule: The hub is the active agent that connects the cabling (= 'a device which connects...'), so the active participle is needed: 'connecting'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 5: Link these statements using a relative clause with a participle

promptParts.before: The technology
promptParts.after: to set up a home network is here today.

questions.grammarRule: Since 'the technology' doesn't need anything itself — it IS needed (by someone) — this requires the passive participle: 'needed to set up...'. (From: 'The technology is here today' + 'It is needed to set up a home network'.)

promptParts.before: You only need one network printer
promptParts.after: to the server.

questions.grammarRule: The printer doesn't connect itself — it IS connected (by someone) — so the passive participle is needed: 'connected to the server'.

promptParts.before: Her house has a network
promptParts.after: basic file-sharing and multi-player gaming.

questions.grammarRule: The network is the active agent that allows file-sharing — active participle: 'allowing basic file-sharing...'.

promptParts.before: There is a line receiver in the living room
promptParts.after: home entertainment audio to speakers.

questions.grammarRule: The receiver is the active agent that delivers audio — active participle: 'delivering home entertainment audio...'.

promptParts.before: Eve has designed a site
promptParts.after: to dance.

questions.grammarRule: The site doesn't dedicate itself — it IS dedicated (by Eve) — passive participle: 'dedicated to dance'.

promptParts.before: She has built in links
promptParts.after: her site to other dance sites.

questions.grammarRule: The links are the active agent that connects the sites — active participle: 'connecting her site to other dance sites'.

promptParts.before: She created the site using a program called Netscape Composer
promptParts.after: in Netscape Communicator.

questions.grammarRule: The program doesn't contain itself — it IS contained (within Netscape Communicator) — passive participle: 'contained in Netscape Communicator'.

promptParts.before: The network can simulate the owner's presence,
promptParts.after: sure vital tasks are carried out in her absence.

questions.grammarRule: The network is the active agent that makes sure tasks are carried out — active participle: 'making sure vital tasks are carried out...'.

promptParts.before: The house has an electronic door-keeper
promptParts.after: to recognize you.

questions.grammarRule: The door-keeper doesn't program itself — it IS programmed (by someone) — passive participle: 'programmed to recognize you'.

promptParts.before: The house has an electronic door-keeper programmed to recognize you,
promptParts.after: access to family only.

questions.grammarRule: The door-keeper is the active agent that gives access — active participle: 'giving access to family only'.
